title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma vs. Bharat Techno Solutions Pvt. Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal
The respondent company failed to comply with the standing orders under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946 while terminating the services of a permanent employee
The petitioner had a clean service record of eleven years and the alleged misconduct was not substantiated by any inquiry report
The termination was retaliatory in nature as it followed the petitioner's complaint to the Labour Commissioner regarding non-payment of overtime wages
The respondent did not produce any documentary evidence before the Labour Court to justify the sudden termination
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi upheld the order of the Labour Court reinstating the petitioner, Ramesh Kumar Sharma, a senior software engineer with eleven years of service, who was wrongfully terminated by Bharat Techno Solutions Pvt. Ltd. The Court found that the termination violated principles of natural justice and was retaliatory. The respondent was directed to reinstate the petitioner with full back wages and continuity of service.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
Writ Petition (Civil) No. 4471 of 2023

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
Versus
BHARAT TECHNO SOLUTIONS PVT. LTD. & ANR. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th September, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY PRAKASH MEHTA

JUDGMENT

1. This writ petition under Article 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India has been filed by the petitioner challenging the order dated 03.01.2023 passed by the Additional Labour Court, Delhi, which had partly allowed the claim of the petitioner but denied full back wages. The petitioner seeks modification of the said order to the extent of granting full back wages from the date of termination.

2. Brief facts as emerging from the record are that the petitioner was employed as a Senior Software Engineer with Respondent No. 1 since April 2011 and was drawing a monthly salary of Rs. 68,500/-. His services were abruptly terminated vide letter dated 17.06.2019 citing vague allegations of gross misconduct and insubordination. No prior show cause notice was issued and no domestic inquiry was conducted before passing the termination order.

3. The petitioner had, approximately three weeks prior to his termination, filed a complaint before the Office of the Labour Commissioner, Delhi, alleging non-payment of overtime wages and arbitrary deductions from salary. The proximity of this complaint to the termination order is a circumstance that cannot be ignored.

4. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Mr. Anil Verma, strenuously urged that the termination was wholly illegal as it was effected without following the mandatory procedure prescribed under the certified standing orders of the establishment. He relied upon the decision of the Supreme Court in Workmen of Firestone Tyre & Rubber Co. of India Pvt. Ltd. vs. Management, (1973) 1 SCC 813, to contend that domestic inquiry is a sine qua non before inflicting the punishment of dismissal on a workman.

5. Respondent No. 1, represented by Mr. Deepak Arora, contended that the petitioner was guilty of serious misconduct and that the termination was justified. However, when called upon to produce the inquiry report or any contemporaneous record establishing the alleged misconduct, the respondent was unable to produce the same before this Court.

6. This Court has carefully considered the submissions advanced by both sides and perused the record. It is a well-settled proposition of law that before a workman of standing is removed from service, the employer must hold a fair and proper domestic inquiry affording the employee a reasonable opportunity to defend himself. The failure to do so renders the termination void ab initio.

7. The retaliatory character of the termination is evident from the chronology of events. The complaint to the Labour Commissioner was filed on 28.05.2019 and the termination followed on 17.06.2019. The respondent has offered no satisfactory explanation for this coincidence.

8. Regarding back wages, this Court is of the considered opinion that denial of full back wages to the petitioner, who has been out of service for over four years through no fault of his own, would be unjust. The petitioner has categorically stated on oath that he remained unemployed during this period and no contrary material has been placed on record by the respondent.

9. In view of the foregoing, this writ petition is allowed. The order of the Additional Labour Court dated 03.01.2023 is modified to the extent that the petitioner shall be entitled to full back wages with continuity of service from the date of termination i.e. 17.06.2019 till the date of reinstatement. The respondent company is directed to reinstate the petitioner within four weeks from today and to pay all arrears of salary within eight weeks.

10. Costs of Rs. 25,000/- are imposed upon Respondent No. 1.

SANJAY PRAKASH MEHTA, J.
SEPTEMBER 14, 2023
